sentence: I have a umbrella.
correct: I have an umbrella.
explanation: Use <an> before words that start with a vowel sound: <an umbrella>.

sentence: She is a honest person.
correct: She is an honest person.
explanation: Use <an> before words that start with a silent 'h': <an honest>.

sentence: He wants to be an doctor.
correct: He wants to be a doctor.
explanation: Use <a> before words that start with a consonant sound: <a doctor>.

sentence: I ate an banana for breakfast.
correct: I ate a banana for breakfast.
explanation: Use <a> before words that start with a consonant sound: <a banana>.

sentence: She is an teacher at my school.
correct: She is a teacher at my school.
explanation: Use <a> before words that start with a consonant sound: <a teacher>.

sentence: I saw a elephant at the zoo.
correct: I saw an elephant at the zoo.
explanation: Use <an> before words that start with a vowel sound: <an elephant>.

sentence: He is a engineer.
correct: He is an engineer.
explanation: Use <an> before words that start with a vowel sound: <an engineer>.

sentence: I need a apple.
correct: I need an apple.
explanation: Use <an> before words that start with a vowel sound: <an apple>.

sentence: She bought the new dress yesterday. It was a beautiful dress.
correct: She bought a new dress yesterday. It was a beautiful dress.
explanation: Use <a> when mentioning something for the first time (not specific).

sentence: I like the dogs. Dogs are friendly animals.
correct: I like dogs. Dogs are friendly animals.
explanation: Do not use <the> when talking about things in general.

sentence: The water is important for life.
correct: Water is important for life.
explanation: Do not use <the> with uncountable nouns when speaking in general.

sentence: I go to the school every day.
correct: I go to school every day.
explanation: We say <go to school> (without <the>) when talking about the regular activity.

sentence: She plays the piano. She also plays the football.
correct: She plays the piano. She also plays football.
explanation: Use <the> with musical instruments but NOT with sports.

sentence: I had a lunch at noon.
correct: I had lunch at noon.
explanation: Do not use articles with meal names: <breakfast>, <lunch>, <dinner>.

sentence: He goes to the bed at 10 pm.
correct: He goes to bed at 10 pm.
explanation: We say <go to bed> (without <the>) when talking about sleeping.

sentence: She is at the home now.
correct: She is at home now.
explanation: We say <at home> (without <the>) when talking about being in your house.

sentence: I go to the work by bus.
correct: I go to work by bus.
explanation: We say <go to work> (without <the>) when talking about the regular activity.

sentence: A sun is very hot today.
correct: The sun is very hot today.
explanation: Use <the> with unique things: <the sun>, <the moon>, <the sky>.

sentence: I can see a moon tonight.
correct: I can see the moon tonight.
explanation: Use <the> with unique things: there is only one moon.

sentence: She is a best student in the class.
correct: She is the best student in the class.
explanation: Use <the> with superlatives: <the best>, <the tallest>, <the most>.

sentence: He is the tall boy. He is taller than me.
correct: He is a tall boy. He is taller than me.
explanation: Use <a> when describing someone in general, not as the only one.

sentence: I want to buy the car someday.
correct: I want to buy a car someday.
explanation: Use <a> when talking about any car in general, not a specific one.

sentence: She has a orange juice every morning.
correct: She has orange juice every morning.
explanation: Do not use <a> with uncountable nouns like <juice>.

sentence: I need an new book for class.
correct: I need a new book for class.
explanation: Use <a> before words starting with a consonant sound: <a new>.

sentence: He is a old man.
correct: He is an old man.
explanation: Use <an> before words that start with a vowel sound: <an old>.
